title: Dyeing with tea and coffee
subtitle: A warm beige to rich brown from kitchen tea and coffee on natural fibres
excerpt: Strong brewed tea and coffee produce soft beige to warm brown tones on wool, silk, and cotton without mordanting. The tannin content of both acts as a natural mordant; the result is gentle and earth-toned, well suited to ageing or antiquing new fabric and yarn.
sourceNotes: Tea and coffee as natural dyes synthesised from widely documented folk dyeing practice. Both are described in general British natural dye resources as mordant-free background dyes suitable for toning or antiquing textiles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A group of astringent plant compounds that bond to protein fibres (wool, silk) and to cotton pre-treated with a protein or mordant. Tea and coffee contain significant tannin, which allows them to dye protein fibres without a separate mordant.
A metallic salt that forms a bond between the fibre and the dye. Tea and coffee are mordant-free dyes because their tannin content provides the bonding chemistry directly.
The pot of water containing the dissolved dye material. For tea and coffee dyeing, the dye bath is simply a very strong brew.

content.text: in sufficient quantity to dye protein fibres (wool, silk) without a separate

is simply a very strong brew: the deeper and more concentrated the tea or coffee, the stronger the colour result. The palette is limited to a gentle range from cream through warm beige to soft brown, but this is exactly the palette needed for ageing or toning new wool that looks too bright.

items.name: Strong black tea (10 to 15 tea bags per litre of water) or strong brewed coffee
Stainless steel pot or dedicated glass bowl

content.text: Brew the tea or coffee very strongly: at least 10 to 15 tea bags per litre of water, steeped for 15 minutes in boiling water, or a quadruple-strength cafetiere of coffee. Strain out the leaves or bags. The deeper the brew colour, the deeper the resulting dye will be.

Allow the dye bath to cool to about 60 degrees Celsius before adding the pre-wetted fibre. Immerse the fibre and hold at 60 degrees Celsius for 30 to 45 minutes, stirring occasionally. The colour builds slowly. For a stronger result, leave the fibre in the dye bath overnight as it cools.

Remove the fibre and rinse in cool water until the water runs clear. Dry in shade. The colour will be slightly lighter once fully dry than when wet. Tea-dyed natural fibres have a warmth that new, bright white yarn loses when aged naturally.

For cotton or linen, a brief soak in strong tea followed by a small alum mordant bath improves the depth and fastness of the colour significantly. The tannin in the tea bonds to the cotton; the alum then fixes additional dye colour on top of the tannin bond.
Spent tea and coffee dye baths can be poured on the garden or composted. No special disposal is needed.

items.problem: The colour is very pale even after a long soak
items.cause: The dye bath was not strong enough
items.fix: Use twice as many tea bags as you think necessary. Tea dyeing builds slowly; a strong initial bath is the most important variable.

items.problem: The colour washes out after the first wash
items.cause: Cotton and linen do not bond well to tannin alone without a mordant
items.fix: For cotton, use an alum pre-mordant (or the soy milk mordant method) before the tea bath. Wool bonds well to tea tannin without a separate mordant.
